questions.question: How many links make up the 2025 AHA universal Chain of Survival?
options: A. Four
B. Five
C. Six
D. Eight
questions.rationale: The 2025 guidelines consolidated adult/pediatric and IHCA/OHCA chains into one universal six-link chain, with a separate Neonatal Chain of Care.

questions.question: Which link of the Chain of Survival does the ACLS provider primarily own?
options: A. Link 1 — activation
B. Link 3 — defibrillation only
C. Link 4 — advanced resuscitation interventions
D. Link 6 — recovery
questions.rationale: ACLS providers primarily deliver Link 4: advanced airway management, IV/IO access, rhythm-specific therapy, medications, and treatment of reversible causes, while also protecting the earlier links.

questions.question: A resident wants to intubate immediately upon arrival at a code, before establishing compressions and pads. What is the correct priority?
options: A. Intubate first to secure the airway
B. Start/continue high-quality compressions and apply the defibrillator first; intubation is layered in without interrupting compressions
C. Intubation always takes priority in ACLS
D. Wait for a physician before doing anything
questions.rationale: Advanced interventions are additive to, not substitutes for, high-quality CPR and early defibrillation, which remain the strongest predictors of survival.

questions.question: What is the primary systems-of-care emphasis for out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (OHCA)?
options: A. Early-warning scores and rapid-response teams
B. Bystander recognition/CPR, dispatcher-assisted instructions, and public-access AEDs
C. Multimodal neuroprognostication
D. ECPR cannulation protocols
questions.rationale: OHCA systems depend heavily on bystanders and dispatch to bridge the gap before EMS/ACLS providers arrive.

questions.question: What is the primary systems-of-care emphasis for in-hospital cardiac arrest (IHCA)?
options: A. Public-access defibrillation programs
B. Surveillance and prevention using early-warning scores and rapid-response teams
C. Community CPR training
D. Regional trauma triage
questions.rationale: IHCA systems focus on catching deterioration early through monitoring and rapid-response systems so that many arrests are prevented before they occur.

questions.question: A patient achieves ROSC after a refractory OHCA in a system with regionalized cardiac arrest centers. What should be considered?
options: A. Transport to the nearest facility regardless of capability
B. Consider triage/transfer to a cardiac arrest center capable of PCI, temperature control, and ECPR
C. Discharge home once a pulse returns
D. Withhold further care since ROSC was achieved
questions.rationale: Analogous to trauma and stroke systems, regionalized cardiac arrest centers with 24/7 PCI, temperature management, and ECPR capability improve outcomes for appropriate patients.

questions.question: Which of the following is an Utstein-style CQI metric?
options: A. Patient's insurance status
B. Chest compression fraction
C. Hospital parking capacity
D. Ambulance color
questions.rationale: Chest compression fraction, time to first shock, time to epinephrine, ROSC rate, survival to discharge, and neurologic outcome are standard Utstein-style metrics used to measure and improve system performance.

questions.question: Why is post-cardiac arrest care described as a continuation of resuscitation rather than a separate phase?
options: A. Because it uses entirely different providers and skills
B. Because the same anticipatory, team-based approach and treatment of reversible causes carries forward after ROSC
C. Because it only involves paperwork
D. Because monitoring stops once a pulse returns
questions.rationale: The 2025 guidelines frame post-ROSC care as continued resuscitation — airway, hemodynamics, temperature control, and cause-directed therapy all extend the same mindset used during arrest.

questions.question: What has replaced the previously separate adult/pediatric and IHCA/OHCA Chains of Survival?
options: A. Four separate specialty chains
B. A single universal six-link chain plus a distinct Neonatal Chain of Care
C. No chain concept is used anymore
D. A three-link simplified chain
questions.rationale: 2025 consolidated the multiple chains into one universal chain applicable across settings and ages, with newborns following a separate Neonatal Chain of Care.

questions.question: A hospital wants to reduce in-hospital arrests. Which system tool most directly supports this goal?
options: A. Public-access AED placement in the community
B. Early-warning scores paired with a rapid-response team
C. A regional ECPR referral network
D. Dispatcher-assisted CPR scripts
questions.rationale: Early-warning scores identify deteriorating inpatients so a rapid-response/medical emergency team can intervene before a full arrest occurs.

questions.question: During a code, the team lead notices the code documenter has not recorded times of drug administration. What system consequence does this create?
options: A. None — timing doesn't matter for CQI
B. It weakens the CQI/Utstein data (e.g., time-to-epinephrine) needed to evaluate and improve system performance
C. It only affects billing
D. It has no effect on patient care or system review
questions.rationale: Accurate timing data underpins CQI metrics like time-to-epinephrine and time-to-shock; without it, systems cannot measure or improve performance.

questions.question: Which best describes the relationship between BLS and ACLS?
options: A. ACLS replaces the need for high-quality BLS
B. ACLS layers advanced interventions onto an uninterrupted BLS foundation of compressions and defibrillation
C. BLS and ACLS are unrelated skill sets
D. ACLS is only used after BLS has failed for 30 minutes
questions.rationale: ACLS providers must protect the BLS foundation (compressions, early defibrillation) while adding airway, IV/IO access, and drug therapy — advanced care is additive.

questions.question: What does the recovery link (Link 6) of the Chain of Survival include?
options: A. Only hospital discharge paperwork
B. Rehabilitation, psychological support, treatment planning, and family support
C. Repeating BLS training annually
D. Nothing beyond ROSC
questions.rationale: Recovery reframes survival as an ongoing process needing rehabilitation and psychosocial support for both the survivor and family, not just a return of pulse.

questions.question: A busy resuscitation team performs a debrief immediately after a code using real-time data. What is the primary purpose of this debrief?
options: A. To assign blame for errors
B. To identify system and team performance gaps and feed lessons back into training/protocols (CQI)
C. To satisfy a legal requirement only
D. To determine reimbursement
questions.rationale: Debriefing is a core continuous-quality-improvement activity, using data from the event to improve future team and system performance.

questions.question: Which statement about advanced airway placement during cardiac arrest is TRUE?
options: A. It should be attempted before starting compressions
B. It must be integrated without causing prolonged pauses in chest compressions
C. It always takes priority over defibrillation
D. It replaces the need for capnography
questions.rationale: Airway management is layered into ongoing compressions; a red flag is any advanced procedure causing a long pause in compressions.

questions.question: Why are trauma and stroke systems of care referenced when discussing cardiac arrest centers?
options: A. They share no relevant similarities
B. Cardiac arrest centers are modeled on the same regionalization principle — routing time-critical patients to specialized capability
C. Cardiac arrest centers replace trauma centers
D. Only rural systems use this model
questions.rationale: Like trauma and stroke systems, regionalized cardiac arrest centers concentrate specialized post-arrest capabilities (PCI, temperature control, neuroprognostication, ECPR) to improve outcomes.
